question: Can epidemic-style R₀ metrics distinguish harmful contamination from beneficial diffusion in knowledge systems, and is the framework externalizable?
expect: Dual R₀ model separates harmful (falsified hub) spread from beneficial (high-cited) diffusion with AUC>0.7. Externalizable template with >=3 non-swarm applications.
actual: PARTIALLY CONFIRMED. R_bad=3.12 (supercritical, correction_rate=0.214 vs need 0.679). R_good diffusion=4.22 (243/249 above critical mass). SIR: 52.3% susceptible, 5.4% infected, 42.4% immune. Classification error dominance discovered: naive FALSIFIED regex gave 122 falsified (15x overcount), changing every derived metric. 4 external applications mapped (social media, supply chain, org knowledge, scientific literature).
diff: Expected clean separation at AUC>0.7. Got the separation BUT discovered classification error dominates spread dynamics — 15x false-positive rate makes all epidemic quantities meaningless. The hard problem is classification, not spread math. This is the externalizable L3 insight: any epidemic model for information systems must solve detection before modeling propagation.

metrics.diagnosis: ALERT: harmful spread is supercritical (R_bad=3.12, correction=0.214, need>0.679) | Beneficial diffusion healthy (243/249 above critical mass) | Low infection rate: 5.4%

classification_error_experiment.intervention_strategy_change: Naive: mass correction campaign needed. Correct: targeted super-spreader correction of 3 nodes.

externalization.core_insight: Classification error dominates spread dynamics. Solve detection before modeling propagation.

prescriptions: Target L-633 (15 uncorrected citers) and L-1192 (12) for immediate correction — these 2 super-spreaders account for 33% of all harmful citations.
Herd immunity requires correction_rate > 0.679; current 0.214 means 3x improvement needed.
Beneficial diffusion is healthy (97.6% above critical mass) — no intervention needed on R_good.
L-601 is maximally trapped knowledge (490 in-deg, 0.05 diffusion) — consider citing its sub-principles directly.
